## [03:02 - 06:07]

powerful and engages the eyes, engages the ears, and really gets people interacting. Now tele-seminars is a little more old school and that's where you're talking on the phone and anywhere from five or ten people to thousands of people can listen to you because of this service, a tele-seminar service, a bridge line they call it. And so with tele-seminars, it's only audio. They can only hear you. With webinars, they can hear you and they can see you. Generally, they can see your computer screen. Maybe you're doing a PowerPoint or a keynote presentation for them. Sometimes the webinars, they can actually see you. You're actually streaming a video feed of you. Maybe your head, your talking head. So anyways, tele-seminars, you can do interviews, you can coach people, you can deliver instructions on how to, you can do product reviews. A lot of different ways you can use tele-seminars and they are so... I mean, they're really pretty simple to do. You sign up for one of those bridge lines. I'll give you some resources in a minute, but there's actually some you can use for free. And then you send people, they get a phone number and they get a pin number and they call up that phone number, enter the pin number and then they're going to be able to listen to you. Tele-seminars are awesomely simple and have a high perceived value. You could just do a tele-seminar series where you get a bunch, six different experts and once a week you interview them. They have a product that can have a very high perceived value. So the best practice is you definitely want to record your call and probably get it transcribed. That's where you basically take the audio and it turns into basically printed words and a PDF and you'll take... And I mean, you can get that stuff transcribed for pretty inexpensive. You want to get real questions from the audience. That's the best. One of the ways to do this is you ask for questions ahead of time. You can use Skype for this. I'll give you some other resources in a minute. And then in terms of what to give the audience, you obviously have to give them the phone number and the pin number for dialing in. You have to give them the exact date and the time. You want to remind them to tweet about it and to add it to Facebook if they've got a social media presence. You want to send email reminders. I like to send one immediately when they sign up, send one the day before the tele-seminar and send one just like 30 to 60 minutes beforehand. You have to remind people or they will forget about it. Be sure to be really specific about what time zone it is. People get confused about time zones all the time, especially if they're calling in from around the world. Let them send their questions in ahead of time. Don't be late for your tele-seminar or your webinar. And you know, basically all of these, we can just... These will all... ## [06:07 - 09:08]

putting in like a seminar or a workshop or a conference. So, not what I would do for my first product, but I just wanted to cover everything for you. They can be really expensive to put on. Amazingly expensive. A lot of times, the hotel... You'll often be doing this at a hotel and they might give you the conference room for free, but then they charge you... You have to like spend a certain amount of money for food and beverages with them and then they just... It's amazing. Like, I recently did a live event and they charged us $70 per gallon of coffee that we served. So, we get coffee, people are coming, you know, drink coffee. $70 per gallon plus they'll usually have an 18 to 20% gratuity plus they'll be a 10 or 11 or 12% tax. And those add on top of each other. So, that's $70. I don't know. It would end up being about $90 a gallon. A gallon for a gallon of coffee. It's just silly. They can be complicated. There's a lot of logistics getting involved, especially if you want to record the event. You want to record the video to turn that into a product. It can get complicated, but generally, if you got the money to spend, then you can get help with all that stuff. But that goes back to being expensive to put on. Now, there are cheaper ways to do it. You might be able to find a local college and use some of their classrooms or meeting space and they can be a lot cheaper. But, you know, they can be complicated and expensive. Would not be the first info product. They can be risky. One of the things like if you get, you know, say, you might think, okay, you're going to spend $3,000 getting some meeting space and you're going to have $10,000 in food and beverage and you're going to have 100 people show up for this event. So, you figure then you've got this $10,000 in food and beverage you've committed to and the $3,000 for the room. So, you've committed to $13,000. It seems like a lot of money, right? Well, let's say it's a two-day event. What you will also often have to do is guarantee a certain number of rooms. So, if there's 100 people showing up for, you know, there's going to be 100 people at the event or you're planning for it and you have room space for 100, then you actually might have to guarantee that you'll have like at least 60 people staying in the hotel for those two nights. So, it's 120 room nights. So, if it's 120 room nights and a hotel room costs $150, you can multiply 120 times 150 and you're basically, I'm going to pull out my calculator here, you'll be guaranteeing those 120 nights times 150, that's $18,000 that you're guaranteeing in room nights. So, if something were to happen and you couldn't have that event, you would not

## [09:08 - 10:45]

only be on the hook for the room, you'd be on the hook for the room, for the food and beverage, and for the hotel room guarantees. So, all of a sudden, you're risking like $30,000 or I've seen $50,000 or $100,000 where you're risking if something happens. If you get in a car accident like the week before and you can't do the event or I actually have a friend who was putting on an event, it was scheduled for I think it was like September 15th and 16th 2001, just like three or four days after 9-11 happened. And if you'll remember when 9-11 happened, there were no planes flying anywhere for the next week, no one could travel. So, he had to cancel his event. So, I mean how can you plan for something like that when you're you know when you're signing a contract? You know, just to wrap it, I mean in that case the hotel actually worked with him and they were able to reschedule it. But, live events can be very risky. But, they can be a great way to create a product. You can record the audio, you can record a video. If you're gonna do that, you need to have some serious preparation ahead of time. You can't just show up at the event and start talking and think it's gonna end up looking like a good product. You can actually pull off a great event and have people think it's a great event, but it won't turn into great video unless you plan out ahead of time.
